items.front: When may mileage-based nonradar procedures and minima be used?
items.back: Only when direct pilot/controller VHF or UHF voice communications are maintained.

items.front: When a position report affecting separation is not received, how soon must action be taken?
items.back: Take action to obtain the report no later than 5 minutes after the aircraft was estimated over the fix.

items.front: When must action be taken for missing position report?
items.back: No later than 5 minutes after the aircraft was estimated over the fix. Per 6-1-2.

items.front: What is the rule on duplicate position reports?
items.back: Do not require an aircraft to make the same position report to more than one facility.

items.front: When do adjacent-airport wake-turbulence minima apply?
items.back: Apply the listed wake-turbulence time minima when an arriving or departing IFR aircraft will cross the flight path of another aircraft that requires wake separation.

items.front: What do the arrival wake-turbulence minima in 6-1-5 apply to?
items.back: They apply to IFR aircraft landing behind other arrivals to the same runway, a parallel runway closer than 2,500 feet, or a crossing runway when projected flight paths will cross.

items.front: What divergence is required to use the initial nonradar departure minima on diverging courses?
items.back: The aircraft must depart the same or adjacent airports and fly courses diverging by 45 degrees or more.

items.front: When can diverging course separation be applied?
items.back: When aircraft fly courses diverging by 45 degrees or more after departing the same or adjacent airports. Per 6-2-1.

items.front: How are same-course departures separated when the following aircraft will climb through the leading aircraft's altitude?
items.back: Use 3 minutes until the following aircraft passes through the leading aircraft's assigned altitude, or 5 miles between eligible DME or ATD aircraft.

items.front: What is same-course climb-through separation?
items.back: 3 minutes until the following aircraft passes through the assigned altitude, or 5 miles between DME-equipped aircraft. Per 6-2-2.

items.front: What is the purpose of the minima in 6-3-1?
items.back: They separate a departure from an arriving aircraft making an instrument approach to the same airport until vertical or lateral separation is achieved.

items.front: What are the basic ways to establish longitudinal separation procedurally?
items.back: Use specified times for departure, arrival at a fix, holding at a fix, or altitude changes at a stated time or fix.

items.front: What speed advantage supports the basic same, converging, or crossing-course longitudinal minimum?
items.back: The leading aircraft must maintain at least a 44-knot speed advantage over the following aircraft for the listed 5-mile or 3-minute minimum.

items.front: What longitudinal separation when leader is 44+ knots faster?
items.back: 5 miles DME or 10 minutes between aircraft without DME/ATD. Per 6-4-2(a).

items.front: How is opposite-course longitudinal separation protected before aircraft pass each other?
items.back: Assign approved vertical separation from 10 minutes before until 10 minutes after the estimated pass, then discontinue it only after one of the paragraph's passing conditions is met.

items.front: What is opposite-course vertical separation?
items.back: Different altitudes from 10 minutes before until 10 minutes after estimated passing time. Per 6-4-3.

items.front: When may pilots maintain longitudinal separation from each other on the same course?
items.back: When the pilots are in direct radio communication, concur, and the following aircraft is authorized to maintain 10 minutes or 20 miles from the leading aircraft.

items.front: When can pilots maintain own longitudinal separation?
items.back: When on the same course in direct radio communication and both concur — 10 minutes or 20 miles DME. Per 6-4-4.

items.front: What must be told to an RNAV aircraft when DME separation is applied along a VOR airway or route?
items.back: Advise the pilot to use DME distances.

items.front: What are the basic methods for procedural lateral separation?
items.back: Use different nonoverlapping airways or routes, different fixes or holding patterns, or specified diverging headings of at least 45 degrees for departures.

items.front: When does lateral separation exist on diverging radials or waypoint tracks?
items.back: When the radials or tracks diverge by at least 15 degrees and either aircraft is clear of the airspace protected for the other.

items.front: If a diverging-radials angle falls between two table values in 6-5-2, which value is used?
items.back: Use the lesser divergence value to obtain the required distance.

items.front: In the 6-5-2 non-DME divergence table, what distance applies at 30 degrees?
items.back: 8 NM.

items.front: When does same-NAVAID radial divergence provide lateral separation?
items.back: When established on radials diverging by at least 15 degrees and either aircraft is clear of the other's protected airspace. Per 6-5-2(a)(1).

items.front: What DME arc minimum applies when both aircraft are 35 miles or less from the NAVAID?
items.back: Use 10 miles between different arcs about the same NAVAID.

items.front: What protected width applies to direct courses or course changes of 15 degrees or less via NAVAIDs or radials at FL600 and below?
items.back: Protect 4 miles on each side of the route out to 51 miles from the NAVAID, then widen on the stated angle out to 130 miles.

items.front: For RNAV diverging or crossing courses, how much nonoverlap is required when the route is 8 miles wide or less?
items.back: Lateral separation exists once the protected airspace no longer overlaps by at least 4 miles.

items.front: When may an altitude be assigned to another aircraft under the basic procedural vertical-separation rule?
items.back: After the aircraft previously at that altitude reports leaving it.

items.front: What changes in the vertical-separation reassignment rule when the 6-6-2 exceptions apply?
items.back: Wait until the aircraft previously at that altitude reports at or passing through another altitude separated by the proper minimum.

items.front: When must extra vertical buffer be applied?
items.back: When severe turbulence is reported or during military aerial refueling. Per 6-6-2(a-b).

items.front: When may pilots maintain vertical separation from each other during climb or descent?
items.back: When both pilots are in direct radio communication, concur, and the lower aircraft is climbing or the upper aircraft is descending.

items.front: When may timed approaches be used?
items.back: They may be used at towered airports with nonradar procedures or radar vectors to final when the listed communication, missed-approach, and procedure conditions are met.

items.front: What is issued to a succeeding aircraft in a timed approach sequence after the preceding aircraft passes inbound over the final approach fix or outer marker?
items.back: Issue the next approach clearance, descent to the vacated altitude, and either a specified leave-fix time or vectors to cross the fix under the paragraph's rules.

items.front: When may an established timed-approach sequence be interrupted?
items.back: Interrupt it when necessary to let an aircraft execute a different type of approach.

items.front: What level-flight restriction applies in IFR weather when 6-7-2b is used?
items.back: Clear the second aircraft early enough to allow at least 1 minute of level flight before it crosses the final approach fix, outer marker, or substitute fix.

items.front: What is the basic minimum interval between successive timed approaches?
items.back: Use 2 minutes or a 5-mile radar interval unless a listed wake-turbulence exception requires more.

items.front: What is the minimum timed approach interval?
items.back: 2 minutes or 5 miles radar between successive approaches. Per 6-7-5(a).

items.front: When is a time check required in timed approaches?
items.back: Issue a time check before specifying a time to leave the approach fix inbound unless the aircraft is being vectored to the final approach course.

items.front: What must be done with the next aircraft if weather suggests the first aircraft may miss the timed approach?
items.back: Issue the next aircraft an alternative missed-approach procedure. If needed, retain radar control or hold remaining aircraft until conditions permit further approach clearances.
